Tweet by @stefanopopoulos (Paras Stefanopoulos)
Author bio: post training @baseten | disciple of history

Created: 2026-04-06T16:32:01.000Z
Metrics: {"retweet_count":12,"reply_count":7,"like_count":99,"quote_count":5,"bookmark_count":164,"impression_count":33504}
Article content: {"entities":{"tweets":[{"id":"2037205514975629493"}],"urls":[{"text":"https://abs-0.twimg.com/emoji/v2/svg/1f49a.svg"}]},"plain_text":"The line in the sand for where RL orchestrators interact with environments has been drawn wrong.
It works great for theoretical applications, however, can cause months of engineering lift for established companies who haven't built 0-1 with RL in mind.
To fit into the current orchestrator-driven paradigm of RL a company needs decouple their action handling code from their product. With the goal of providing a nice sandboxed interface, exposing clean APIs for performing actions on state.
Environment-driven training is a solution for performing RL with minimal or no engineering lift for a company's product.
This architecture interacts with a product via the same mechanisms as a user. No sandboxed, fine grained APIs needed to support every tool call.
This is exactly what Cursor has done with their new Composer model

 
To articulate the environment-out approach, lets take theoretical company A.
Company A offers a document editor, and an LLM agent which can be called to help make decisions on grammatical errors, or edit the document.
Orchestrator-Driven Reinforcement Learning
 
This is the common RL setup. You have a server for training, a server for inference and an orchestrator which sits in the middle. The orchestrator coordinates these different components to perform rollouts and trigger weight updates.
Here is the typical life-cycle of a rollout:
 
The key is that the orchestrator also responsible for managing the sandboxes. Spinning up a new sandbox for each rollout, parsing the tool call requests, and performing the actions on the sandbox.
What makes this difficult for customer A, is that they will need to nicely package up their entire backend into a sandbox environment, which can be controlled via API requests.
/read_file
/apply_diff
/collect_grammatical_recommendations
/accept_grammatical_recommendation
...
Ideally, it would even run on the orchestration machine, so we would need a docker image.
Environment-Driven Reinforcement Learning
 
We add a proxy in-front of our inference server which records traces. The orchestrator interacts with the production service as if we were a user.
"Hey Company A, I want to create a document"
Send Company A LLM agent, "I want to do X, Y"
Company A runs their agent against our LLM using their production system, avoiding the need for a nicely packaged sandbox
We record the trace in the recording proxy
The production agent runs as usual, but rolls out against our LLM, behind the recording proxy.
In the case that the rollout involves writing to the environment in ways which will impact future rollouts, we usually need a “replay” mechanism to be available. We have found this to be more common in products than we initially thought.
A replay mechanism is anyway to reproduce a given state within a product, i.e. cloning a coding project, creating a new PR/repo, starting a document from a historical checkpoint.
We now need some special reward shaping as we don't have access to the final environment state in the sandbox. We have been able to derive great signal using only our recorded traces.
Here is the life-cycle of an environment-driven rollout:
 
Extending to Continual Learning
The obvious extension to this is online-live reinforcement learning. It's easy to imagine a world where your production model, is that which is served in the inference server.
The orchestrator produces rewards from live traces, ships them to training, then updates the weights of the inference server. Voilà - a self-upgrading model, learning from your production usage.
This is what Cursor has done with their new Composer model.
 
Wrapping Up
Environment-driven training is an approach we often take when customers want to prove the value of a custom model, but want to minimize the engineering up-lift needed.
The orchestrator interacts with the production system in the same way a user would. We then derive signal from the production system's interactions with our inference server.
This is a simple change in information flow, but we haven't seen the distinction made explicit.
We constantly evolve our systems to derive signal in the face of multitudes of constraints. This is an elusive problem, and the community is converging.
If you want to join the fight, we are doing this and much more at Baseten. We're hiring 💚","title":"Environment-Driven Reinforcement Learning","cover_media":"3_2040178360186437632","preview_text":"The line in the sand for where RL orchestrators interact with environments has been drawn wrong.
It works great for theoretical applications, however, can cause months of engineering lift for","media_entities":["3_2040117019044114432","3_2040116735400202242","3_2040167378479394816","3_2040183731416408064"]}
